sentence: Please turn of the lights before you leave.
correct: Please turn off the lights before you leave.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <turn off> (not <turn of>). <Off> means to stop.

sentence: She picked on the phone and called her mother.
correct: She picked up the phone and called her mother.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <pick up> (not <pick on>). <Pick up> means to lift.

sentence: He needs to look of his little sister.
correct: He needs to look after his little sister.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <look after> (not <look of>). It means to take care of.

sentence: Can you turn in the TV? I want to watch the news.
correct: Can you turn on the TV? I want to watch the news.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <turn on> (not <turn in>). <On> means to start.

sentence: She put of her jacket because it was cold.
correct: She put on her jacket because it was cold.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <put on> (not <put of>). <Put on> means to wear.

sentence: I need to look up my keys. I can't find them.
correct: I need to look for my keys. I can't find them.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <look for> (to search). <Look up> means to search in a reference.

sentence: He took of his shoes at the door.
correct: He took off his shoes at the door.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <take off> (not <take of>). <Take off> means to remove.

sentence: Please sit up. There is a chair for you.
correct: Please sit down. There is a chair for you.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <sit down> (not <sit up>). <Sit down> means to take a seat.

sentence: She woke down early this morning.
correct: She woke up early this morning.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <wake up> (not <wake down>). <Wake up> means to stop sleeping.

sentence: He gave in smoking last year.
correct: He gave up smoking last year.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <give up> (not <give in>). <Give up> means to stop doing something.

sentence: Can you write up your phone number for me?
correct: Can you write down your phone number for me?
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <write down> (not <write up>). <Write down> means to note on paper.

sentence: She ran on her old friend at the supermarket.
correct: She ran into her old friend at the supermarket.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <run into> (not <run on>). It means to meet by chance.

sentence: Please throw out the rubbish before you leave.
correct: Please throw away the rubbish before you leave.
Please throw out the rubbish before you leave.
explanation: Both <throw away> and <throw out> are correct and mean to discard.

sentence: He got of the bus at the wrong stop.
correct: He got off the bus at the wrong stop.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <get off> (not <get of>). <Get off> means to leave a vehicle.

sentence: She came up home very late last night.
correct: She came back home very late last night.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <come back> (not <come up>). <Come back> means to return.

sentence: I need to fill up this form for the doctor.
correct: I need to fill in this form for the doctor.
I need to fill out this form for the doctor.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <fill in> or <fill out> (not <fill up>). They mean to complete a form.

sentence: He broke up his car on the way to work.
correct: His car broke down on the way to work.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <break down> (not <break up>). <Break down> means to stop working.

sentence: She looked up the children while their parents were away.
correct: She looked after the children while their parents were away.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <look after> (not <look up>). It means to take care of.

sentence: Please calm up. Everything will be fine.
correct: Please calm down. Everything will be fine.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <calm down> (not <calm up>). It means to become less upset.

sentence: He set down a new business last year.
correct: He set up a new business last year.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <set up> (not <set down>). <Set up> means to establish.

sentence: She carried up with her work despite the noise.
correct: She carried on with her work despite the noise.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <carry on> (not <carry up>). <Carry on> means to continue.

sentence: I need to sort up this mess in my room.
correct: I need to sort out this mess in my room.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <sort out> (not <sort up>). <Sort out> means to organize.

sentence: He got in the bus at the station.
correct: He got on the bus at the station.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <get on> (not <get in>) for buses and trains.

sentence: She stood down when the teacher entered the room.
correct: She stood up when the teacher entered the room.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <stand up> (not <stand down>). <Stand up> means to rise to your feet.

sentence: He held up the phone. Don't hang up!
correct: He held on the phone. Don't hang up!
Hold on the phone. Don't hang up!
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <hold on> (not <hold up>). <Hold on> means to wait.
